title: Hyperthermia / Heat Stroke / Heat Related Sickness
synonyms: Hyperthermia / Heat Stroke / Heat Related Sickness

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Heat stroke is an excessive elevation of core body temperature due to failure of thermoregulation. Heat stroke should be suspected when symptoms do not settle promptly with rest and removal from heat stress, or when neurological dysfunction is present. Heat-related illness may be caused by environmental exposure, heavy exertion, hot or humid conditions, drugs, infection, or impaired heat dissipation.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Heat Exhaustion (core temperature 37°C to less than 40°C)
- Severe headache and/or dizziness (especially postural)
- Diaphoresis, nausea and vomiting
- Tachypnoea, tachycardia, hypotension (especially postural)
- Muscle pain, fatigue, cramps
- Heat Stroke (core temp ≥ 40°C)
- CNS dysfunction with bizarre behaviour, seizures and altered level of consciousness
- Extreme fatigue, headache and/or syncope
- Facial flushing, vomiting and diarrhoea
- Skin hot, possibly dry or nil diaphoresis
- Dysrhythmia and hypotension
- Tachypnoea and acute respiratory distress syndrome
- Hypoglycaemia and hyperkalaemia

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Hyperthermia is a medical emergency; rapid cooling should begin as soon as practicable and should not be delayed while waiting for transport
- In the early stages of heat stroke, CNS dysfunction may be the first sign of thermoregulatory failure
- The young and elderly are more prone to heat-induced illness
- Tympanic temperature may underestimate true core temperature in heat illness
- Assess patients left in cars or enclosed hot environments carefully for heat-induced illness
- Cardiac dysfunction and tachyarrhythmias often improve with cooling and supportive care
- Avoid shivering where possible, as it increases heat production
- Antipyretic medications (e.g. paracetamol, ibuprofen) do not treat environmental or exertional hyperthermia and should not be used for temperature control in heat stroke; this does not apply to infective fever, where antipyretics remain appropriate

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Move the patient to a cool environment or shade when safe to do so
- Position patient supine if appropriate
- Vital Sign Survey, including close monitoring of temperature and BGL where available
- Consider Oxygen if indicated
- Provide water only if the patient is fully conscious and not at risk of aspiration

**Apply critical treatment points:**
  - Remove excess clothing
  - Start rapid active cooling immediately
  - If feasible and safe, consider whole-body cold-water immersion for severe exertional heat stroke
  - If immersion is not feasible, use evaporative cooling with water spray plus fanning, and apply ice packs to the neck, axillae, and groin
  - Continue cooling during preparation for transport and during transport where possible
  - Do not delay ambulance activation, but do prioritise early cooling

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Obtain a 12 lead ECG
- If unconscious, seizing, or unable to protect the airway, consider advanced airway management
- Establish vascular access
- Consider fluid therapy if indicated, including cooled IV fluids where practical
- Consider antiemetic if required
- Continue active cooling during transport wherever possible

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Seizures can occur in severe hyperthermia
- Heat syncope: fainting due to peripheral vasodilation in a hot environment
- Heat cramp: exercise-associated muscle cramping, often with salt and fluid depletion
- Heat exhaustion: salt and/or water depletion with nausea, vomiting, fatigue, and excessive sweating; tissue injury does not usually occur
- Heat stroke: hyperthermia with neurological dysfunction due to failure of thermoregulation
